speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 12-19-'95. I'm interviewing Moses Essig, Moishe Essig. Leah Zazulyer is my name. This is Rochester, New York, USA. And we're using English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Leah Zazulyer. I'm interviewing Moses Essig in Rochester, New York, in the United States. And this is 12-19-'95. I didn't do that in order, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Essig, would you tell us your name and how you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moses Essig, E-S-S-I-G. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you spell 'Moses' when you were in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. No, no. I spell in the Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moishe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: M-O-I-S-H-E.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 80 years-- 79.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Cracow or in a little--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Cracow. Tell me about your life before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Life before the war was pretty good. No problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was your family like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same-- there was a life. They were-- they were-- they was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was work-- he was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother help him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We helped out each other, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a store, or did he work for other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. My father was every Friday go out and sell them, sell outside. He was a tailor for people, too. They come to him. They get him to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did he, he go on the streets? Or he went to other cities or towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. It was a market. It was a [INAUDIBLE] a market. Twice a week, we got the market.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brothers, we got four, five, six-- six brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: And three sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So altogether you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: 11 People.

speaker: interviewer
text: 11 people. And nine of you were brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sisters. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you, at the first, or the last, or in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the middle. [LAUGHS] I come from a twin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and did the twin live?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that a boy, or a girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: A boy, a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he die of?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a little boy, he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about six, seven months. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, Jewish, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you went to school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Hebrew school or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. It's a Polish school. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years did you go to, to that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10 years, about 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- what was it that you liked to do in your spare time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Play soccer. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a big field to play it in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, we got a big field. Oh, ho ho. Big field, big, big.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in Cracow or outside of Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, in Cracow. The second street from me, the kids go play ball-- soccer game.

speaker: interviewer
text: What street did you live on?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a house, an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where a lot of people were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a courtyard?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not with a courtyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, with all those brothers and sisters, did you play games or do things together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they got separated, I got separated. We play outside sometimes, outside. And after this I go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working by, how they call them, the sweetness-- chocolate, yeah, cookies, a bakery, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do the baking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I do that, too. After this, when the war come out, go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think about your childhood, what, what are some of the things you remember about your family? Holidays, or going to synagogue--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we got holidays. Oh, yeah. Come night for Passover, we got together. The whole family get together, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowhere, and staying home. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did grandparents come to visit you, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. The grandparents was-- lived far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Galicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do when you all got together for the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: We go to the temples. And after this, we go eat. And they'd say, go have a good time. Go out. Take the fresh air. When you baked things at the bakery, did you have special favorite things that you liked to bake? No, no, no, no. We not special people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother bake-- yeah, my mother baked lots-- lot of stuff. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Today nobody do nothing. The American people no like the baking, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you were growing up? Just before the war started, what, what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the bakery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were about how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: This time I was about 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have girlfriends then, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Already? No, no, no, no, no. Not a girlfriend here. No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you travel anywhere, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I wasn't home. My girlfriend got friends. Even a girl, was good to go out, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you when the Germans came into Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In home. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? Do you remember what it felt like when they-- you first saw them come?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they come on in my home town. They say, out, out, out, out, out out. First, they don't say nothing. No, no. They, they was happy they come inside. And after this, oh, we have to go. After little later, they open up the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you saw them first, were they coming down the street? Where you in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [INAUDIBLE]. When they coming in, I move out. I move out to the-- far away from them. A couple miles from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the farm. Me, my father and mother, the whole family go to a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: A farm. A Polish farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a long time. My father was a tailor. He make them something. So everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family knew Polish people in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where they his customers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not neighbors. Just customers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was this farm owner a customer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he invite you? Or you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've got customers, and they come to us. And my father make a tailor for them, do everything for them. That's how.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they say, come to us because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they know us. They told-- they told us come over to-- with us for a while. Not to go someplace else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you, as a boy, experienced any antisemitism in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like we'd go some place and start fighting, I tell them, why, I fix you up for good. And I fix them up for good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: We take a couple the people. We in a straight inside, and we make it for them. We wait for him, all the guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We beat them up. And police come right way. A lot of police. Oh, it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you always win?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to win.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever beat you up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. Some other guys, other people. We go after him, after the guys. Like, like we the Nazis. It's the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so when the police would come, what would happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They arrested the guy, a couple guys, and we go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: They never arrested you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I got business with the police. Even over here, I got business with the police. They come to me, need something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went to the farm--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was-- that was because you knew the Germans were coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was already. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days after they came did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: A month, two months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like during that two months when they first came?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, let me tell you this, there was all the kind Germans. The good Germans, and the bad Germans. Like over here, good people, bad people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did they first do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, they just come in, look around, everything, and that's all. And they say, you have to go work. I work by the German too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Asphalt, asphalt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Asphalt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we go work with them. After this, we go work off the airport. We got a big airport. And we work with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how long did you work for them before you went to the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is after the farm. We go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you went to the farm and you stayed there about a month?

speaker: interviewee
text: A month, maybe. After they go some place else, then we have to go back. Where else did you go?

speaker: interviewer
text: Another city-- another little county. Yeah. Another customer of your father's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do while you were on the farms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They called, the German comes over, they tell you you have to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made you come back to Cracow, or you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But they tell me go to work, and I tell them to go back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did your whole family go back to Cracow then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. My brothers and my sisters, they no was in Cracow. They was in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: They went into Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Germans came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows?

speaker: interviewer
text: And so everybody went back except your, your brother and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Your sis-- my sisters and my brothers. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many of you went back to Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six people, six, seven people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my home town, in the house. We got the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was empty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and waiting for you? OK, and then you worked for the Germans. And did your father work for them, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father continue to be a tailor for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he teach me. I told him, never mind. It's finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after you were working and working for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I worked, they put it in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did it happen? Did they come to the house, or did they get you on the street or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, see, they come to our house. They tell them the Jewish organization, the committee, you have to go work. They got an order, after they need the people. We go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were filling a quota?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. And after this, we'll go to their-- the, the camps. He would come of the people, tell them, hey, we need people. And this time, was actually Mr. Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He go to Plaszow, where I was. I needed people. No, no, no. First is go to Hitler. The [INAUDIBLE], Mr. Schindler. Need to tell why you kill the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we'll get to that. We're getting you to Plaszow right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow, Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Plaszow, excuse me. OK, so how did you go from Cracow to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they take us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a truck or a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: In trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In trucks. Were they all young people? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah. A lot of them. Mostly were young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: All men, boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men, womans. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took your whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the whole family. Me, my brother, my sister, they go someplace else. And me with my brother, we go to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: The parents were still that-- the parents, I think they took it someplace else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long the trip was it from Cracow to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: A half hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was in, in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's about '41. It's a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was not-- it was still a labor camp then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got there, and what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We got happened. We get that work, we have to go work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I mean, you went into the camp. Was it an open gate?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was the SS-- the SS, the soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: The soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: They watch that, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Local soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Local soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not, not SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS, too, together. Because I tell you what. The SS, the soldiers, they no like the SS. Sure, yeah, they was enemies with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Right. So what was your living conditions like at Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camps?

speaker: interviewer
text: In that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the guys not so good like in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a, a criminal. They was a criminal. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many criminals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Many criminals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, big, all around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were all together with the criminals.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I'm a criminal, too. I was a criminal, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: But a different kind, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: A different kind of criminal, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were Christians there and Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. All the kind nationality. Christian, Jews, all another one, religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep in barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: On straw, or on wooden beds?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wooden bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the food like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not like over here, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, for example.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soup. And see, in Plaszow, there were different kind. And now after this, they move-- they build a new building for the people. That's all. And after this, there's-- when there was the war, an American plane fall down on the camp. Yeah. Fall down on our camp, then the SS looking at the-- all the people here. Oh, big one. Big, big.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They fall down, and then nothing happened. A good thing nothing happened. Here come the Germans. They take away the two pilots with the plane. They give them respect salute, and they take away them. yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows what's going to happen to them? What's happened to them?

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were alive when the plane--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. They was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They [INAUDIBLE] with the German. Look the German told me, look, Amerikaner. I ask him, you like them? Sure. Don't think the German like the Germans. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of work did you do in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] work. Dishes, dishes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah ha, you cleaned.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I work with them. It was hard, terrible, ooh boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of hard work was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dishes. You make dishes. Plates.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was for Schindler, or in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Not for Schindler. Schindler come late.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Plaszow, you made dishes also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what part of the job did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same by the oven. Take them out, put it in. Like a bakery.

speaker: interviewer
text: All day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who, from your family was with you in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Michael?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We both were together from first until finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about how long were you in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cracow?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow. In Plaszow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, about two, three years. And after this, Schindler had come-- he come. Schindler had come, and had tell he wants some people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now during the two or three years that you were there, was there a change when more Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. No change. Was the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your life was the same there all those-- those couple years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing you can do. You have to do it what they say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing big happened that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother also?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. We go together. When he-- we-- if he was going, I'll go with him. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so when Schindler came into the camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did he pick you? And, and did you pick your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I tell you what. What's happened like-- for Schindler, you see, Schindler come. He go to the bus when it come from the camp. He tell them, I want people. And that way, a friend of mine, he called, come on, come on, come on. Come on. He took me, and after, he took my brother. A lot of people took from the camp. We need the people. Not [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] we go to the [INAUDIBLE], to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And other people in Plaszow were making bombs, munitions, and things like that. Did you ever do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bombs?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, or munitions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You didn't do that in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Krakow, we didn't make ammunition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just did the bakery things? Or the, the dishes.

speaker: interviewee
text: All [INAUDIBLE] the dishes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about how many people did Schindler take from Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: He took about 2,000 people. Man and woman. The womans, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that, that business of being taken by him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [INAUDIBLE] All of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he choose you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He'd-- he come over there, and he'd tell that he wants the people. He wants the people get to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who chose you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He chose himself. [INAUDIBLE] that the foreman was coming. He'd tell ever-- come on let's go. Go, go, go, go, go, go. We need-- somebody looking for you. I was this time. My brother, together. He push me. I push him. And a friend of mine, told him to go on. Go over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wanted to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I will go over there. No, nothing I can do. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] See, when I wouldn't go to him, they'd be gone by a long time away. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got on a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a, a train was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, a train for the-- for us, a person plane. Like all the time, the planes. The train-- the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: A person train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not A cattle train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. After there, we go someplace else. They give us the cattle planes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went for how long until you got to Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- about how long from Plaszow to Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Plaszow to Gross Rosen, I thinks that we go two, two days. I'm not sure. We was in Gross Rosen, we stay all of overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gross Rosen? Ah. I don't want to tell nothing about what's going on. It was worse over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's what's important for you say, is what you remember about--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was bad, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: We sleep on the floor. Overnight, and on the morning, he comes and tell them, come on! Get the people. Come on, come on, come, on. No hold them up. Let's go. He took a man and a woman. Sure. We got about, about 3,000 people. He saved 3,000 people, he saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I talked to him, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Well what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brunnlitz. We talked to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean in Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Not with him, no. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Saw, saw him in, in, in Czechoslovakia. Over there, and I talked to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there you were sleeping on the floor in, in Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Gross Rosen, we just sleeping on the floor. [INAUDIBLE] because come middle of the night, or at in the morning, I tell them, come on. Get the people out. Sure. Come the other way, take out the people. He sits over there night time, night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else was bad about Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was bad about Gross Rosen? Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see what's going on. They kill people in the oven, put on the bakery. Sure, they're putting the people right in the bakery. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you got on the train the next day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then who was go to Schindler, he tell them, there in Gross Rosen don't touch the people. Not the men, not the woman. They're mine's, and I take back. Yeah. In the morning, he take everybody, everybody who belonged to him, and going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So other people were killed at Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not the people that he said don't touch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. But about 4,000 people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Was supposed to talk about the situation was. Was supposed to talk all night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we got time.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to go to home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else you want to say about Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: So what-- I say a lot of friends, Polacks, Polish people-- I ask, what are you doing over here? Oh, they'll take me, too. You want to go with me? Where? To Schindler. No, I have to go home back. There a lot of international people. Was German even, Russian, all that kind. What they living in their hometown someplace. It's not if [INAUDIBLE] No, no. She went over there like that. But they took a big shower-- from the shower, straight to the oven. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Young people, kids, everything. Pregnant woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not a good thing, believe me, not to tell what's going on. What's what over there. Ah, what it can do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wish to say, the young people, the young generation over here, to watch your steps, and be ready. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They think I left. I not left. We see that look like now. You see, Europe, what's going on again. Better in a wish, another war comes up in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. So, all right. We'll stop here now. OK. We'll stop and--
